questions.question:
Group 1 contains some CPU scheduling algorithms and Group 2 contains some applications. Match entries in Group 1 to entries in Group 2.

Group I:
(P) Gang Scheduling
(Q) Rate Monotonic Scheduling
(R) Fair Share Scheduling

Group II:
(1) Guaranteed Scheduling
(2) Real-time Scheduling
(3) Thread Scheduling

A. P-3, Q-2, R-1
B. P-1, Q-2, R-3
C. P-2, Q-3, R-1
D. P-1, Q-3, R-2
questions.to_find: Correct matching of CPU scheduling algorithms to their application domains

understand.plain: Three CPU scheduling algorithms are given in Group I and three application domains are given in Group II. We need to match each algorithm to the application domain it is designed for. Gang Scheduling schedules threads of a parallel process together, Rate Monotonic is for real-time systems, and Fair Share ensures proportional CPU allocation across user groups.

keywords.explain: A scheduling strategy where all threads of a process are scheduled to run simultaneously on multiple CPUs, used in parallel/multi-threaded computing.
keywords.example: A multi-threaded simulation whose 4 threads all get CPUs at the same time slot.

keywords.explain: A fixed-priority real-time scheduling algorithm where tasks with shorter periods get higher priority, guaranteeing deadlines for periodic real-time tasks.
keywords.example: A sensor task running every 10 ms gets higher priority than a display task running every 50 ms.

keywords.explain: A scheduling policy that distributes CPU time fairly among groups of users or processes, guaranteeing each group a proportional share.
keywords.example: If two users share a system, Fair Share ensures each user group gets 50% CPU regardless of how many processes each runs.

understand.visual_alt: Three algorithms: Gang Scheduling maps to Thread Scheduling (3), Rate Monotonic maps to Real-time Scheduling (2), Fair Share maps to Guaranteed Scheduling (1).

given.aim: Match each scheduling algorithm in Group I to its correct application domain in Group II.

terms.meaning: Schedules all threads of a process concurrently on multiple processors
terms.example: Used in parallel computing for thread coordination
terms.connects: Its primary application is Thread Scheduling (3) — it manages execution of thread groups together

terms.meaning: Fixed-priority preemptive algorithm for periodic real-time tasks; shorter period = higher priority
terms.connects: Classic Real-time Scheduling (2) algorithm with proven schedulability bounds

terms.meaning: Allocates CPU proportionally among user groups, guaranteeing each group its share
terms.connects: Implements Guaranteed Scheduling (1) by ensuring fair CPU distribution to groups

given.plan: Recall the purpose of each scheduling algorithm. Gang Scheduling is for thread groups (Thread Scheduling). Rate Monotonic is the classic real-time algorithm (Real-time Scheduling). Fair Share guarantees proportional CPU to groups (Guaranteed Scheduling).

steps.title: Match Gang Scheduling
steps.apply: Gang Scheduling schedules all threads of a parallel process simultaneously on multiple CPUs. Its domain is Thread Scheduling. So P -> 3.
steps.note: Gang scheduling is used in multi-processor/parallel systems to reduce thread synchronization delays.

steps.title: Match Rate Monotonic Scheduling
steps.apply: Rate Monotonic Scheduling assigns priorities based on task periods (shorter period = higher priority). It is the fundamental algorithm for Real-time Scheduling. So Q -> 2.
steps.note: RMS is provably optimal among fixed-priority preemptive algorithms for periodic real-time tasks.

steps.title: Match Fair Share Scheduling
steps.apply: Fair Share Scheduling divides CPU among user groups, guaranteeing each group its proportional share. This maps to Guaranteed Scheduling. So R -> 1.
steps.note: Fair Share prevents one group from monopolizing CPU — a form of guaranteed service.

steps.title: Form the complete match
steps.apply: P-3, Q-2, R-1. This corresponds to option A.
steps.note: All other options incorrectly pair Rate Monotonic with Thread Scheduling or Gang with Guaranteed Scheduling.

questions.formula_note: Knowledge of scheduling algorithm purposes: Gang Scheduling for threads, Rate Monotonic for real-time, Fair Share for guaranteed CPU allocation.

questions.question:
An operating system uses Shortest Remaining Time first (SRT) process scheduling algorithm. Consider the arrival times and execution times for the following processes:

Process | Execution Time | Arrival Time
P1      | 20             | 0
P2      | 25             | 15
P3      | 10             | 30
P4      | 15             | 45

What is the total waiting time for process P2?

A. 5
B. 15
C. 40
D. 55
questions.to_find: Total waiting time for process P2 under SRT (Shortest Remaining Time) scheduling

understand.plain: SRT (also called SRTF - Shortest Remaining Time First) is the preemptive version of SJF. At each moment, the process with the smallest remaining burst time runs. When a new process arrives, if its burst time is less than the current process's remaining time, a preemption occurs. We need to track when P2 is waiting (in ready queue but not running) and sum those intervals.

keywords.explain: Preemptive scheduling where at any point, the process with the least remaining execution time runs. New arrivals can preempt the running process.
keywords.example: If Process A has 10 units left and Process B arrives with 5 units, B preempts A.

keywords.explain: Total time a process spends in the ready queue waiting for CPU (not including its own execution time or time before arrival).
keywords.example: If P2 arrives at t=15, finishes at t=55, and has burst=25, waiting time = 55-15-25 = 15.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='200' height='28' class='ok' rx='3'/><text x='110' y='39' text-anchor='middle' fill='white' class='tag'>P1: 0-20</text><rect x='210' y='20' width='100' height='28' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='260' y='39' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>P2: 20-30</text><rect x='310' y='20' width='100' height='28' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='360' y='39' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>P3: 30-40</text><rect x='410' y='20' width='150' height='28' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='485' y='39' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>P2: 40-55</text><rect x='560' y='20' width='50' height='28' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='585' y='39' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>P4...</text><text x='10' y='70' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='205' y='70' class='sub ink'>20</text><text x='305' y='70' class='sub ink'>30</text><text x='405' y='70' class='sub ink'>40</text><text x='555' y='70' class='sub ink'>55</text><text x='10' y='95' class='sub mut'>P2 waits: 30-40 = 10 units (P3 preempts). Total wait = 15.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Gantt chart: P1 runs 0-20, P2 runs 20-30, P3 preempts P2 at 30 and runs 30-40, P2 resumes and runs 40-55. P2 waiting time = 10 units (30 to 40) + 5 units (15 to 20) = 15.

given.aim: Simulate SRT scheduling and compute total waiting time of P2.

terms.meaning: P1 is the only process initially, so it runs from t=0.
terms.connects: P1 has 5 units remaining when P2 arrives at t=15; since 5 < 25, P1 continues.

terms.meaning: P2 arrives at t=15 with burst=25. P1 has only 5 remaining at that point.
terms.connects: P2 does not preempt P1 (P1 remaining=5 < 25), waits 15-20, then runs 20-30, then is preempted by P3 at t=30.

terms.meaning: P3 arrives at t=30 with burst=10. P2 has 20 remaining.
terms.connects: P3 (10) < P2 (20), so P3 preempts P2. P2 waits from 30 to 40.

terms.meaning: P4 arrives at t=45 with burst=15. P2 has 15 remaining at that point.
terms.connects: Tie between P2 (15) and P4 (15). P2 (already running) continues. P2 finishes at t=55.

given.plan: Simulate the SRT schedule step by step tracking each process's remaining time at each event. Then compute P2 waiting time = completion time - arrival time - burst time.

steps.title: t=0 to t=15: P1 runs
steps.apply: Only P1 is available (arrived at t=0, burst=20). P1 runs. At t=15, P2 arrives with burst=25. P1 remaining = 20-15 = 5. Since P1 remaining (5) < P2 burst (25), no preemption. P1 continues.
steps.note: SRT preempts only if new arrival has LESS remaining time than current process.

steps.title: t=15 to t=20: P1 completes; P2 waits
steps.apply: P2 waits in the ready queue from t=15 to t=20 (5 units of waiting). At t=20, P1 finishes. P2 is the only process, so P2 starts running.
steps.note: P2 first waiting interval: 15 to 20 = 5 units.

steps.title: t=20 to t=30: P2 runs
steps.apply: P2 runs from t=20. At t=30, P3 arrives with burst=10. P2 remaining = 25-10 = 15... wait: P2 ran from 20 to 30 = 10 units. P2 remaining = 25-10 = 15. P3 burst = 10. Since P3 (10) < P2 remaining (15), P3 preempts P2.
steps.note: Preemption occurs when newly arrived process has smaller remaining time.

steps.title: t=30 to t=40: P3 runs; P2 waits again
steps.apply: P3 runs from t=30 to t=40 (burst=10). P2 waits in ready queue from t=30 to t=40 = 10 units of waiting.
steps.note: P2 second waiting interval: 30 to 40 = 10 units.

steps.title: t=40 to t=55: P2 resumes and completes
steps.apply: At t=40, P3 finishes. P2 resumes with 15 units remaining. At t=45, P4 arrives with burst=15. P2 remaining = 15-5 = 10 units... wait: P2 ran from 40 to 45 = 5 units. P2 remaining = 15-5 = 10. P4 burst = 15. P2 (10) < P4 (15), so no preemption. P2 continues and finishes at t=40+15 = t=55.
steps.note: At t=45, P2 remaining is 10, P4 is 15 — P2 continues without preemption.

steps.title: Calculate P2 total waiting time
steps.formula_raw: Waiting time = Completion time - Arrival time - Burst time
steps.apply: P2 completion = 55, arrival = 15, burst = 25. Waiting time = 55 - 15 - 25 = 15 units. Verification: P2 waited 15-20 (5 units) + 30-40 (10 units) = 15 units. Correct.
steps.note: Both methods (formula and direct counting of wait intervals) give 15.

solution.result: B. 15 — P2's total waiting time is 15 units.

questions.formula_note: Waiting time = Completion time - Arrival time - Burst time; SRT preempts when a new arrival has shorter remaining time than the currently running process.

questions.question:
In the following process state transition diagram for a uniprocessor system, assume that there are always some processes in the ready state:

(Diagram shows states: Start -> Ready <-> Running -> Terminated, Running -> Blocked -> Ready)
Transitions: A (Start->Ready), B (Ready->Running), C (Running->Ready), D (Running->Blocked), E (Blocked->Ready), F (Running->Terminated)

Now consider the following statements:
I. If a process makes a transition D, it would result in another process making transition B immediately.
II. A process P2 in blocked state can make transition E while another process P3 is running.
III. The OS uses preemptive scheduling.
IV. The OS uses non-preemptive scheduling.

Which of the above statements are TRUE?

A. I and II
B. I and III
C. II and III
D. II and IV
questions.to_find: Which statements about process state transitions are TRUE for the given uniprocessor system

understand.plain: A uniprocessor process state diagram has transitions between Start, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Terminated states. We need to determine which statements about these transitions are correct. Transition D (Running->Blocked) frees the CPU, transition C (Running->Ready) represents preemption, and transition E (Blocked->Ready) is triggered by an I/O completion event, which can happen while another process is running.

keywords.explain: The OS can forcibly remove a running process from the CPU (transition C: Running->Ready) to give CPU to another process.
keywords.example: In Round Robin, a timer interrupt causes preemption after a time quantum.

keywords.explain: A process enters blocked state (transition D: Running->Blocked) when it requests I/O or waits for an event. It cannot run until the event occurs (transition E: Blocked->Ready).
keywords.example: Process reading from disk moves to Blocked; when disk I/O completes (interrupt), it moves back to Ready.

180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='bg'/><text x='200' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Ready</text><ellipse cx='350' cy='50' rx='55' ry='25' class='ok'/><text x='350' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>Running</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='140' rx='50' ry='25' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Blocked</text><ellipse cx='490' cy='50' rx='60' ry='25' class='bg'/><text x='490' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Terminated</text><line x1='105' y1='75' x2='148' y2='57' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='110' y='68' class='sub mut'>A</text><line x1='250' y1='50' x2='292' y2='50' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='268' y='44' class='sub mut'>B</text><line x1='350' y1='75' x2='260' y2='128' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='318' y='110' class='sub mut'>D</text><line x1='200' y1='115' x2='200' y2='78' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='208' y='100' class='sub mut'>E</text><line x1='323' y1='38' x2='252' y2='38' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='285' y='32' class='sub mut'>C</text><line x1='405' y1='50' x2='428' y2='50' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='414' y='44' class='sub mut'>F</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#5F5E5A'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Process state diagram: Start->Ready (A), Ready->Running (B), Running->Ready (C, preemption), Running->Blocked (D, I/O wait), Blocked->Ready (E, I/O complete), Running->Terminated (F).

given.aim: Determine which statements about process state transitions are true given the diagram and the assumption that there are always processes in the ready state.

terms.meaning: A running process is moved back to the ready queue — this is preemption.
terms.example: Timer interrupt in Round Robin causes preemption.
terms.connects: Existence of transition C in the diagram implies the OS supports preemptive scheduling.

terms.meaning: Running process makes an I/O request and waits, freeing the CPU.
terms.example: Process calls read() — it blocks until data arrives.
terms.connects: When D occurs, the CPU is free. Since always some process is in the ready state, another process will be dispatched (transition B). But is it IMMEDIATE?

terms.meaning: I/O completion (interrupt) moves blocked process back to ready queue.
terms.example: Disk read completes; interrupt handler wakes the waiting process.
terms.connects: E is triggered by an external event (interrupt), which can happen at any time — even while another process is running.

terms.meaning: If D occurs, does B happen immediately?
terms.connects: D frees CPU but the OS may not immediately dispatch (could do some overhead first). Statement I says IMMEDIATELY — this is not guaranteed. The OS scheduler might have overhead before dispatching. So Statement I is not necessarily TRUE.

given.plan: Analyze each statement using knowledge of OS scheduling mechanics. Transition C implies preemption (Statement III true). Transition E can occur while a process runs (Statement II true). Statement I's 'immediately' is too strong. Statement IV contradicts C.

steps.title: Analyze Statement I
steps.apply: Statement I: If a process makes transition D (Running->Blocked), another process makes transition B (Ready->Running) immediately. When D occurs, the CPU becomes free. The OS scheduler will pick a process from the ready queue. However, 'immediately' is problematic — there is always scheduler overhead (context switch overhead). More importantly, this statement tries to say D necessarily causes an immediate B, but the OS could have dispatcher delays. In GATE context, Statement I is considered FALSE because in a non-preemptive scheduler, after D, the OS might wait or there's scheduling overhead. The key issue: Statement I is about whether B is IMMEDIATE — not guaranteed.
steps.note: Statement I is FALSE — 'immediately' is too strong; there may be scheduling overhead or the OS may not guarantee instant dispatch.

steps.title: Analyze Statement II
steps.apply: Statement II: A process P2 in blocked state can make transition E (Blocked->Ready) while another process P3 is running. Transition E is triggered by an I/O completion interrupt, which is an asynchronous event. This can happen at any time, including while P3 is executing on the CPU. The interrupt handler runs and moves P2 from Blocked to Ready queue. This is TRUE — blocked-to-ready transitions are interrupt-driven and happen concurrently with CPU execution.
steps.note: I/O completion interrupts are asynchronous — they can fire while any process is running on the CPU.

steps.title: Analyze Statement III
steps.apply: Statement III: The OS uses preemptive scheduling. The diagram includes transition C (Running->Ready), which represents a process being forcibly removed from the CPU and placed back into the ready queue. This transition exists only if the OS supports preemption (e.g., timer-based preemption). Since the diagram shows transition C, Statement III is TRUE.
steps.note: If the OS were purely non-preemptive, transition C (Running->Ready) would not exist in the diagram.

steps.title: Analyze Statement IV
steps.apply: Statement IV: The OS uses non-preemptive scheduling. This directly contradicts Statement III, and transition C in the diagram shows preemption is possible. Statement IV is FALSE.
steps.note: Statements III and IV are mutually exclusive; since C exists, III is true and IV is false.

steps.title: Identify correct option
steps.apply: True statements: II and III. This corresponds to option C.
steps.note: C. II and III is the answer.

solution.result: C. II and III — Statement II (blocked process can transition E while another runs) and Statement III (OS uses preemptive scheduling) are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: Process state transition model: transition C (Running->Ready) indicates preemptive scheduling; transition E (Blocked->Ready) is interrupt-driven and asynchronous, occurring while another process runs on CPU.
